chunks.text: **Acute coronary syndrome (ACS / MI)** — chest pain or pressure lasting >10 min, often substernal, radiates to left arm / jaw / back. Associated: diaphoresis, nausea, dyspnea, syncope. Risk factors: age >45 M / >55 F, DM, HTN, smoking, dyslipidemia, family history, prior CAD. **Must rule out first.** Immediate: 12-lead ECG within 10 min, serial troponins q3h, aspirin 325 mg chewed unless contraindicated, oxygen if SpO2 <94%, nitroglycerin if no RV infarct or PDE5 use. Source: ACEP Acute Chest Pain Clinical Policy.

**Aortic dissection** — sudden tearing / ripping pain, radiating to back between scapulae. BP differential >20 mmHg between arms, pulse deficit, new aortic regurgitation murmur, widened mediastinum on CXR. Risk: uncontrolled HTN, Marfan / Loeys-Dietz / Ehlers-Danlos, pregnancy third trimester, cocaine, bicuspid aortic valve. **Avoid thrombolytics if suspected.** Confirm with CT angio aorta. Immediate: control HR <60 (esmolol / labetalol) then BP <120 systolic.

**Pulmonary embolism** — pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, tachycardia, sometimes hemoptysis. Unilateral leg swelling / calf tenderness = DVT. Risk: recent surgery / immobility / long flight, OCP / HRT / pregnancy, active malignancy, prior VTE, thrombophilia. Wells score: >6 high, 2–6 moderate, <2 low + PERC rules out if all negative. Confirm: CT pulmonary angiography or V/Q if renal fail / contrast allergy. Treat: anticoagulation + thrombolytics if massive (hemodynamic instability).

**Tension pneumothorax** — sudden severe chest pain + dyspnea, tracheal deviation away from affected side, absent / decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance, hypotension, JVD, cyanosis. **Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for CXR.** Immediate needle decompression: 14G at 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular OR 4th/5th intercostal anterior axillary. Follow with tube thoracostomy.

**Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome)** — severe retrosternal chest or epigastric pain immediately after forceful vomiting. Subcutaneous emphysema (crepitus on neck / chest), Hamman's sign (crunch on auscultation), mediastinal air on CXR, pleural effusion. Confirm with gastrografin contrast esophagram or CT. Mortality high if >24h delay. Surgical consult STAT + broad-spectrum abx + NPO.

**Pericarditis / cardiac tamponade** — sharp positional chest pain worse lying flat, relieved leaning forward. Pericardial friction rub. ECG: diffuse ST elevation with PR depression (pericarditis). Tamponade = Beck's triad: hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds + pulsus paradoxus. Echo confirms effusion. Tamponade = urgent pericardiocentesis.

**GERD / costochondritis** (diagnoses of exclusion only after ACS/PE/dissection ruled out) — retrosternal burning relieved by antacids, reproducible chest wall tenderness on palpation (costochondritis), no exertional component. **Do NOT anchor to these until serious causes excluded.**

**Pediatric fever — high-risk groups** (WHO IMCI). Age <3 months with any fever (rectal ≥38°C) → full sepsis workup regardless of appearance (blood + urine + CSF, empiric abx). Age 3–36 months: toxic appearance, petechial rash, bulging fontanelle, stiff neck → LP + meningitis coverage. Tachypnea RR>60 infants / >40 toddlers = pneumonia / sepsis. SpO2 <92% → admit. Poor feeding / lethargy / capillary refill >3s = compensated shock.

**Geriatric polypharmacy encounter** — always ask: how many prescribers? who filled when? any recent hospital discharge? over-the-counter / supplements? Beers Criteria screens: anticholinergic load, long-acting benzo, NSAIDs if CKD, first-gen antihistamines, muscle relaxants. STOPP/START framework: check for under-prescribing (statin post-MI, anticoag post-stroke, ACEi in HF). Red flag combos: warfarin + new antibiotic, metformin + contrast, SSRI + tramadol.

**Large-vessel occlusion stroke** — sudden focal neuro deficit. FAST: Face droop, Arm weakness, Speech slurred, Time to call. NIHSS ≥6 suggests LVO. Time windows: tPA <4.5h from last known well (strict contraindications), mechanical thrombectomy <24h if LVO on CTA. Immediate: non-contrast CT to rule out hemorrhage, then CTA head+neck, glucose, BP control (target <185/110 pre-tPA).

**Sepsis / septic shock** — suspected infection + qSOFA ≥2 (SBP<100, RR≥22, altered mental status) OR SIRS ≥2 (fever, tach, tachypnea, WBC shift). Septic shock = persistent hypotension requiring pressors after 30 mL/kg fluid + lactate >2. Hour-1 bundle: lactate, blood cx × 2, broad-spectrum abx, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, reassess, initiate pressors if MAP <65.

**SEPSIS RED FLAG.** Trigger: infection suspicion + ≥2 of (T<36 or >38.3°C, HR>90, RR>20, WBC<4 or >12, altered mental status). Septic shock = hypotension SBP<90 after 30 mL/kg fluid + lactate >2. Hour-1 bundle: (1) lactate; (2) blood cultures × 2 BEFORE abx; (3) broad-spectrum empiric abx; (4) 30 mL/kg crystalloid if lactate ≥4 or hypotensive; (5) vasopressors (norepi first-line) if MAP <65 post-fluid. Source MIR: SSC 2021.

**STEMI RED FLAG.** Criteria: ≥1 mm ST elevation in 2 contiguous leads OR new LBBB with Sgarbossa criteria OR posterior STEMI (reciprocal depression V1-V3). Door-to-balloon <90 min (PCI) or door-to-needle <30 min (fibrinolytics if PCI delayed >120 min). Immediate meds: aspirin 325 mg chewed, P2Y12 (ticagrelor/clopidogrel), heparin / LMWH, high-intensity statin. Avoid NG if RV infarct (inferior MI + RV leads) or PDE5 use within 24h.

**STROKE / LVO RED FLAG.** FAST positive + time of last known well. tPA window <4.5h (strict exclusions: recent surgery, anticoag, prior ICH, active bleed, BP >185/110). Thrombectomy window <24h if proximal LVO on CTA. Door-to-CT <25 min. BP target pre-tPA: <185/110 (labetalol or nicardipine). Glucose check — exclude hypoglycemia mimic. NIHSS, CT head non-contrast, CTA head+neck.

**HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY.** BP >180/120 + end-organ damage (chest pain, stroke symptoms, pulmonary edema, acute kidney injury, eclampsia). Goal: reduce MAP 20–25% over first hour (NOT more — risk of watershed ischemia). Agents: nicardipine or clevidipine IV drip (safe all comers), labetalol (avoid if bradycardic / severe asthma / cocaine), esmolol if aortic dissection. **Avoid rapid drops.** Asymptomatic severe HTN (urgency) = oral, outpatient — NOT emergency.

**PEDIATRIC DANGER SIGNS (IMCI).** Any of: (1) unable to drink or breastfeed, (2) vomits everything, (3) convulsions, (4) lethargy / unconscious, (5) severe respiratory distress (chest indrawing, stridor at rest, grunting), (6) cyanosis / SpO2 <92%, (7) pallor, (8) capillary refill >3s. Any danger sign = ADMIT + IV access + empiric abx if <3m or sepsis suspicion. Add: stiff neck / bulging fontanelle = meningitis workup.

**ANAPHYLAXIS.** Likely when acute onset + ≥2 of: (a) skin/mucosal (hives, swelling), (b) respiratory (dyspnea, wheeze, stridor), (c) CV (hypotension, syncope), (d) GI (persistent vomiting, cramping). **IM epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg (0.01 mg/kg peds) anterolateral thigh IMMEDIATELY** — don't wait for IV. Repeat q5 min if no response. Adjuncts: antihistamines (second-line only), steroids, IVF. Biphasic risk — observe 4–6h minimum.

**DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS.** Triad: hyperglycemia (>250) + ketosis (β-hydroxybutyrate >3 or urine ketones) + anion-gap metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, HCO3 <18). Common in new T1DM or infection / non-adherence. Treatment: (1) IVF — 1L NS then match losses; (2) potassium — check before insulin, hold insulin if K<3.3; (3) insulin — IV drip 0.1 U/kg/h after K checked; (4) search for precipitant (infection, MI, non-adherence).

**MASSIVE / SUBMASSIVE PE.** Massive = PE + hemodynamic instability (SBP <90 or drop >40). Submassive = PE + RV dysfunction (echo / troponin / BNP). Massive: consider systemic thrombolytics (tPA) if no absolute contraindications. Submassive: heparin bridge → DOAC, consider catheter-directed if deteriorating. Bedside echo: RV dilation, McConnell's sign.

**ACUTE GI BLEED.** Hematemesis / melena / hematochezia + tachycardia or hypotension = hemodynamically significant. Management: (1) 2 large-bore IV; (2) type & cross 4 units; (3) transfuse if Hgb<7 (or <8 if cardiac); (4) IV PPI bolus + drip if UGIB suspected; (5) octreotide if variceal source; (6) GI consult for endoscopy <24h. NG lavage not routine.

**ADULT MENINGITIS.** Classic triad: fever + neck stiffness + altered mental status (only ~45% have all three). Petechial / purpuric rash → meningococcal. Immediate: blood cultures, empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin; add ampicillin if >50 / immunocompromised for Listeria), dexamethasone 10 mg IV BEFORE or with first abx. LP unless contraindicated (focal deficits, immunocompromised, seizure → CT first). Do NOT delay abx for LP.

**Warfarin + antibiotics**: TMP-SMX, ciprofloxacin, metronidazole, and azoles amplify INR via CYP2C9 inhibition and gut flora disruption. Bleeding risk up to 3× baseline. Action: check INR 48–72h after abx start, reduce warfarin ~25–50% preemptively if starting TMP-SMX or metronidazole, consider alternative abx where possible (doxycycline, cephalexin, nitrofurantoin lower interaction). Source: AGS Beers + Lexicomp Level X warnings.

**Metformin + IV iodinated contrast**: risk of contrast-induced nephropathy → lactic acidosis. Hold metformin day of contrast and for 48h after. **Resume only after confirmed normal renal function (Cr / eGFR return to baseline).** If eGFR <60 pre-contrast → hold and consult. If emergency contrast needed in renal patient: supplemental hydration + NAC not proven. Alternative: MR contrast without iodine if available.

**Ciprofloxacin + QT-prolonging agents**: fluoroquinolones prolong QT. Combined risk with amiodarone, sotalol, dofetilide, methadone, ondansetron, haloperidol, TCAs, macrolides. Torsades de pointes risk, especially with hypokalemia / hypomagnesemia. Action: baseline ECG before ciprofloxacin if on any QT-prolonging drug, correct electrolytes (K>4, Mg>2), consider alternative abx (doxycycline, Bactrim if compatible). Avoid combining with Class III antiarrhythmics.

**SSRI / SNRI + tramadol / triptans / MAOI / linezolid**: serotonin syndrome risk — autonomic instability, clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia, altered mental status. Can be fatal. Action: avoid combination; if unavoidable, use lowest effective dose and monitor closely. Wait 2 weeks between MAOI and SSRI (5 weeks for fluoxetine). Tramadol is particularly insidious — often prescribed for pain in depression patients.

**ACE-inhibitor / ARB + potassium-sparing diuretic**: hyperkalemia risk, can precipitate arrhythmia. Combinations: lisinopril/losartan + spironolactone / eplerenone / amiloride / triamterene. Also watch ACE + K supplement + NSAID ("triple whammy"). Action: check baseline K and Cr, recheck 1–2 weeks after initiation. Hold if K>5.5. Use spironolactone cautiously in HFrEF (beneficial at 25–50 mg but monitor).

**AGS Beers Criteria — high-risk meds in elderly (≥65)**: avoid first-gen antihistamines (diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine) — anticholinergic, falls. Avoid long-acting benzos (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) — falls, cognitive impairment; short-acting (lorazepam, oxazepam) preferred at low dose. Avoid muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol) — sedation, falls. Avoid NSAIDs if CKD / HF / anticoag. Avoid sliding-scale-only insulin without basal. Avoid PPI >8 weeks without indication (fractures, C diff).

**Renal dose adjustment — common meds**:
- **Metformin**: hold if eGFR <30; use caution 30–45.
- **Gabapentin**: 300 mg BID–TID if CrCl 30–60; 200 mg qhs if <15.
- **Enoxaparin (prophylactic)**: 30 mg q24 if CrCl <30 (not 40 q24). Therapeutic: 1 mg/kg q24 if CrCl <30.
- **Vancomycin**: target trough 15–20 for deep infections; AUC 400–600. Adjust by nephrology nomogram if CrCl <50.
- **Nitrofurantoin**: avoid if CrCl <30 (reduced efficacy + toxicity).
- **NSAIDs**: avoid in CKD stage 3+.
- **DOACs**: apixaban preferred in CKD stage 4 (2.5 mg BID if 2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60, Cr ≥1.5); avoid dabigatran and edoxaban in CrCl <30.

**Missing aspirin in suspected ACS**: aspirin 162–325 mg chewed is a Class I indication for suspected ACS / STEMI. Absolute contraindications rare: active bleed, severe asthma w/ prior ASA-induced bronchospasm, known allergy. If clinician has not administered aspirin in an ACS-suspect encounter → flag as missing guideline-directed therapy.

**Anticoagulation + bleeding risk markers**: active peptic ulcer, recent GI bleed (<6m), platelets <50, uncontrolled HTN >180/110, falls (HAS-BLED ≥3), recent intracranial bleed, active malignancy with invasive procedures. **Reassess HAS-BLED at each encounter.** Reversal: warfarin → vitamin K + PCC; DOAC → specific reversal (idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet for Xa inhibitors) OR PCC if unavailable.

**Opioid prescribing red flags**: concurrent benzodiazepine (respiratory depression, 10× overdose death), concurrent gabapentinoids, MME >50/day (CDC 2022 guideline), untreated OSA, CKD (morphine / codeine metabolites accumulate — prefer hydromorphone or fentanyl), hx SUD. Always co-prescribe naloxone if MME ≥50 or benzo concurrent. Check state PDMP.

**Teratogenic meds to screen in repro-age female**: ACEi/ARB (renal dysgenesis), warfarin (embryopathy), isotretinoin (category X), valproate (neural tube + behavioral), methotrexate, statins (avoid), lithium (Ebstein anomaly), tetracyclines after 15 weeks, fluoroquinolones (avoid if alternative), topiramate (cleft). Verify pregnancy status + contraception counseling at visit.
